question.text: Meera invested in ELSS (Equity Linked Savings Scheme) under the old tax regime. Which taxation regime does ELSS follow?

answer.text: EEE — contributions are deductible, growth is exempt, and redemption is exempt
EET — contributions are deductible under Section 80C, growth is exempt, but redemption gains are taxed as capital gains
ETE — contributions are deductible, growth is taxed annually, and redemption is exempt
TET — contributions are not deductible, growth is exempt, and redemption gains are taxed

explanation.text: **The Logic:** ELSS follows the EET (Exempt-Exempt-Taxed) regime under the old tax system: (1) First E: Contributions qualify for deduction under Section 80C (up to Rs. 1.5 lakh); (2) Second E: The growth/accumulation within the fund is not taxed annually; (3) T: On redemption, gains are taxed — LTCG above Rs. 1.25 lakh is taxed at 12.5% under Section 112A. **The Trap:** EEE describes PPF where even maturity is exempt — ELSS gains are taxable on redemption. ETE describes tax-saving FDs where interest is taxed annually. TET would apply if there were no Section 80C deduction — this is actually what ELSS becomes under the new regime (where 80C is unavailable). **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A MFD exam extensively tests the EET classification of ELSS versus other tax-saving instruments. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When comparing ELSS with PPF for clients on the old regime, highlight that ELSS has a shorter lock-in (3 years vs 15 years) but gains are taxable (EET vs EEE) — the trade-off is liquidity versus tax efficiency.
